message:
bd-kh2.2: E2E RPC session connector tests + bead hygiene

Add 23 E2E RPC session connector tests covering get_state,
set_session_name, set_thinking_level, get_messages, get_session_stats,
get_last_assistant_text, set_model error paths, unknown commands,
response ID echo, malformed JSON recovery, and full multi-command
lifecycle. Close parent beads bd-3r75, bd-23ko, bd-26s (all children done).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1av0","title":"Epic: Node.js Stdlib Shim Implementation — complete pi:node/* polyfill surface","description":"# Goal
Implement the full set of Node.js standard library shims (pi:node/*) so that existing npm extensions written for Node.js can run in the PiJS QuickJS runtime without modification.

# Background
The PiJS runtime (src/extensions_js.rs) runs extension JavaScript in QuickJS, which has NO Node.js stdlib. Extensions that import from "fs", "path", "url", "os", "crypto", "http", "buffer", "events", or "stream" fail immediately.

Current state:
- bd-1h06 covers node:child_process shim (the ONLY shim with a dedicated bead)
- bd-354t covers pi.* connector shims (different: PI-specific APIs, not Node compat)
- bd-1gbi covers MAPPING missing shims — but mapping is analysis, not implementation
- The compatibility scanner (src/extensions.rs) already DETECTS Node.js imports and reports them — it just cant fix them

Shim strategy per EXTENSIONS.md: each pi:node/* module routes through hostcalls to Rust implementations. For example, node:fs.readFile() calls pi.tool("read", ...) hostcall internally. This keeps the security model intact — all filesystem access goes through capability-gated dispatcher.

# Why This Matters
- The conformance corpus has 60+ extensions; many are npm packages that assume Node.js stdlib
- Without shims, compat scanner reports "unsupported import: fs" and extension fails
- THIS is the primary reason conformance tiers 3-5 are #[ignore] — multi-file extensions with npm deps need these shims
- Every major extension ecosystem (VSCode, Obsidian) has Node.js stdlib assumptions baked in

# Architecture
Each shim follows this pattern:
1. QuickJS module registered via Module::declare() in PiJsRuntime
2. Module exports match Node.js API surface (function signatures, return types)
3. Implementation delegates to hostcalls: pi.tool() for file ops, pi.exec() for process ops, pi.http() for network
4. Synchronous Node APIs (e.g., fs.readFileSync) shimmed via blocking hostcall bridge
5. Each shim has coverage matrix documenting which APIs are implemented vs stubbed

# Prioritization (by extension corpus usage frequency)
Tier 1 (blocks most extensions): node:fs, node:path, node:url
Tier 2 (blocks many extensions): node:os, node:crypto, node:buffer
Tier 3 (blocks some extensions): node:events, node:http/https, node:stream

# Dependencies
- Depends on bd-1gbi (Map missing shims) completing first to finalize exact API surface needed
- Relates to bd-xgo (extc pipeline) which handles import rewriting: import "fs" -> import "pi:node/fs"
- Relates to bd-29fu (compat scan on expanded corpus) which will validate shim coverage

# Children (8 shim subtasks)
One bead per stdlib module, each self-contained with its own API surface spec","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:35:02.997560439Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:08.855436321Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["compatibility","extensions","shims"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1av0","depends_on_id":"bd-1gbi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:12.605728908Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0","depends_on_id":"bd-xgo","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:20.001366859Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":501,"issue_id":"bd-1av0","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ARCHITECTURAL CONTEXT: The shim strategy is: each pi:node/* module is registered in QuickJS via Module::declare() in src/extensions_js.rs. Pure-JS shims (path, url, events, buffer) are embedded as JS source strings. I/O shims (fs, crypto, http) route through hostcalls to Rust.

PRIORITIZATION RATIONALE: node:fs and node:path are Tier 1 because they appear in >80% of npm extensions. node:os and node:crypto are Tier 2 (30-50%). node:events and node:http are Tier 3 (10-30%). This is based on import frequency analysis from the conformance corpus.

CRITICAL DESIGN DECISION: Sync functions (readFileSync, etc.) MUST block the QuickJS event loop until the hostcall completes. This is intentional — Node.js sync functions block, and extensions expect this behavior. The blocking is contained to the QuickJS thread (doesn't block the main Rust event loop).

EXISTING PARTIAL WORK: Phase 5 community conformance (bd-2ru2) already added fragments: realpathSync, mkdirSync, promises namespace, hostname. This epic COMPLETES these partial implementations to full API surface.

DEPENDENCY NOTE: This epic depends on bd-1gbi (Map missing shims) to finalize the exact API surface needed. If bd-1gbi hasn't completed, individual shim tasks can still proceed based on the standard Node.js API — the mapping just helps prioritize which APIs to implement first.

ESTIMATED EFFORT: node:fs (12-16h), node:path (3-4h), node:crypto (6-8h), node:os (2-3h), node:url (3-4h), node:buffer (6-8h), node:events (3-4h), node:http (10-14h). Total: ~45-61 engineering hours.","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:02:28Z"},{"id":514,"issue_id":"bd-1av0","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (EPIC-LEVEL):
- [ ] All 10 subtasks (9 shims + integration tests) closed
- [ ] process.env, process.cwd(), process.platform work (blocks 80%+ of corpus)
- [ ] node:fs read/write/stat/readdir/exists work via hostcalls
- [ ] node:path, node:url, node:buffer, node:events work as pure JS
- [ ] node:crypto hash/UUID work via Rust delegation
- [ ] node:http/https make requests via pi.http hostcall
- [ ] Cross-shim interop validated (45+ integration tests)
- [ ] At least 3 previously-failing conformance extensions now pass with shims
- [ ] Quality gates: cargo fmt, cargo check, cargo clippy, cargo test all pass
- [ ] All shim APIs documented in EXTENSIONS.md","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:08Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1av0.1","title":"Implement node:fs shim — filesystem operations via pi.tool hostcalls","description":"# Goal
Implement the node:fs (and node:fs/promises) shim module for PiJS QuickJS runtime, routing all filesystem operations through the capability-gated pi.tool("read"/"write") hostcalls.

# Background
node:fs is the MOST imported Node.js module in the extension corpus. Without it, any extension that reads config files, writes output, or checks file existence fails immediately. The existing PiJS runtime (src/extensions_js.rs) already has partial node:fs support added during Phase 5 community conformance (bd-2ru2): realpathSync, promises namespace, mkdirSync. This task completes the full surface.

# API Surface (prioritized by corpus usage)

## Tier 1 — Must Have (blocks 80%+ of extensions using fs)
- readFileSync(path, encoding?) → string | Buffer
- writeFileSync(path, data, encoding?)
- existsSync(path) → boolean
- readFile(path, encoding?, callback) — async version
- writeFile(path, data, encoding?, callback)
- statSync(path) → Stats object { isFile(), isDirectory(), size, mtime }
- stat(path, callback)
- readdirSync(path) → string[]
- readdir(path, callback)
- mkdirSync(path, { recursive }) — already exists, verify completeness
- unlinkSync(path) — delete file
- rmdirSync(path) — delete directory

## Tier 2 — Should Have (blocks 30%+ of extensions)
- promises.readFile / promises.writeFile / promises.stat / promises.readdir
- realpathSync(path) — already exists
- renameSync(oldPath, newPath)
- copyFileSync(src, dest)
- accessSync(path, mode) — check permissions
- createReadStream(path) — returns readable stream (depends on node:stream shim)
- createWriteStream(path)
- watchFile(path, callback) — polling file watcher

## Implementation Strategy
- Each sync function: blocking hostcall to Rust → pi.tool("read", { path }) or pi.tool("write", { path, content })
- Each async function: Promise-based hostcall (same as sync but non-blocking)
- Stats object: construct from tool result metadata (size, mtime from read tool details)
- existsSync: try-catch wrapper around statSync (return false on error)
- Encoding parameter: "utf8"→string, null/buffer→Buffer shim

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs: Register node:fs module, implement shim functions
- May need to add new tool operations for stat/unlink/rename (currently tools.rs has read/write/edit but not stat-only or delete)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All Tier 1 functions implemented and tested
- [ ] Tier 2 functions implemented where feasible
- [ ] Sync functions block the JS event loop correctly
- [ ] Async functions use Promises (no callback hell)
- [ ] Stats object matches Node.js shape (isFile, isDirectory, size, mtime)
- [ ] All operations go through capability-gated hostcalls (no direct filesystem access)
- [ ] At least 5 conformance corpus extensions that previously failed on fs now pass","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:55:55.946664883Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:55:55.946664883Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","filesystem","shims"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:55:55.946664883Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1av0.10","title":"Tests: Node.js shim integration suite — cross-shim interop and conformance validation","description":"# Goal
Comprehensive integration test suite that validates ALL Node.js shims work correctly both individually and in combination. This is the final validation gate before the Node.js shim epic can be considered complete.

# Background
Each individual shim bead (bd-1av0.1 through bd-1av0.9) includes its own unit tests. This bead adds INTEGRATION tests that verify cross-shim interop and real-world extension compatibility. Without this, individual shims might pass but break when used together.

# Test Categories

## 1. Cross-Shim Interop Tests (15+ tests)
These verify that shims work correctly TOGETHER:
- fs.readFile + path.resolve → read file at resolved path
- fs.writeFile + Buffer.from → write binary data
- crypto.createHash + fs.readFile → hash file contents
- http.get + url.parse → fetch from parsed URL
- events.EventEmitter + process.on → event chain works
- os.tmpdir + fs.writeFile + path.join → write to temp directory
- Buffer.from(fs.readFileSync(path)) → binary file round-trip
- process.env.HOME + path.join + fs.existsSync → check home dir file

## 2. Real Extension Replay Tests (10+ tests)
Take REAL code patterns from the conformance corpus and verify they work:
- Pattern: const configPath = path.join(process.env.HOME, '.config', 'ext.json')
           if (fs.existsSync(configPath)) { ... }
- Pattern: const hash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(content).digest('hex')
- Pattern: const url = new URL(endpoint); url.searchParams.set('key', process.env.API_KEY)
- Pattern: const emitter = new EventEmitter(); emitter.on('data', (chunk) => { ... })

## 3. Error Path Tests (10+ tests)
- fs.readFileSync on nonexistent file → throws ENOENT
- crypto.createHash with unsupported algorithm → throws error
- process.exit during hostcall → clean shutdown, no crash
- Buffer.from with invalid encoding → throws TypeError
- http.get with invalid URL → error event fired
- path.resolve with no cwd hostcall available → fallback behavior

## 4. Performance Tests (5+ tests)
- 1000 sequential fs.readFileSync calls → complete within 5s
- 100 concurrent crypto.createHash operations → no deadlocks
- process.env access 10000 times → cached, sub-1ms per call
- Buffer.alloc(10MB) → completes without OOM
- EventEmitter with 1000 listeners → no performance cliff

## 5. Conformance Regression Tests (5+ tests)
- Re-run previously-failing extensions from conformance corpus
- Verify they now pass with shims enabled
- Track which tiers are unblocked by shim completion

# Logging Requirements (per bd-4u9 convention)
Every test MUST emit structured JSONL logs:
{
  "test": "cross_shim_fs_path_resolve",
  "timestamp": "2026-...",
  "inputs": { "relative_path": "./config.json", "cwd": "/project" },
  "expected": "/project/config.json",
  "actual": "/project/config.json",
  "pass": true,
  "duration_ms": 12,
  "shims_exercised": ["node:fs", "node:path"],
  "artifacts": ["test-config.json"]
}

# Test Infrastructure
- Use TestHarness from tests/common/ for JSONL logging
- Use tempdir for filesystem isolation
- Use VCR cassettes for HTTP shim tests
- No mock libraries (project convention)
- All tests deterministic (DeterministicClock for timers)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 45+ test cases covering all 5 categories
- [ ] All tests emit structured JSONL logs
- [ ] Cross-shim interop validated for all pairwise combinations
- [ ] At least 3 previously-failing conformance extensions now pass
- [ ] No mock libraries used
- [ ] All tests pass cargo test
- [ ] Performance baselines documented","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:56:46.180146279Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:37.319091655Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","shims","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:56:46.180146279Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:35.795189003Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:35.985034958Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:36.174351857Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:36.364901103Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:36.557250464Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:36.747984955Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:36.937288710Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:37.132664592Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.10","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:57:37.319028267Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1av0.2","title":"Implement node:path shim — path manipulation utilities (pure JS)","description":"# Goal
Implement the node:path shim module. This is one of the simplest shims because path operations are pure string manipulation — no hostcalls needed.

# Background
node:path is the second most imported Node.js module. It provides cross-platform path manipulation utilities. Since these are pure functions with no I/O, the shim can be implemented entirely in JavaScript within QuickJS — no Rust bridge needed.

# API Surface (complete — all are pure functions)
- path.join(...segments) → string
- path.resolve(...segments) → string (needs cwd from hostcall)
- path.dirname(p) → string
- path.basename(p, ext?) → string
- path.extname(p) → string
- path.normalize(p) → string
- path.isAbsolute(p) → boolean
- path.relative(from, to) → string
- path.parse(p) → { root, dir, base, ext, name }
- path.format(pathObject) → string
- path.sep → "/" (Pi runs on Unix)
- path.delimiter → ":" (Pi runs on Unix)
- path.posix → self (Pi is always POSIX)
- path.win32 → stub that throws (Pi doesnt support Windows paths)

# Implementation Strategy
- Pure JavaScript implementation registered as QuickJS module
- Only path.resolve() needs a hostcall (to get cwd) — cache cwd at module load time
- All other functions are simple string operations
- Test against Node.js path module output for 50+ edge cases

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs: Register node:path module with JS source

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All listed functions implemented
- [ ] Matches Node.js behavior for edge cases (empty strings, trailing slashes, .. traversal)
- [ ] path.resolve uses real cwd from runtime

 {"id":"bd-1av0.3","title":"Implement node:crypto shim — hashing, random UUID, and basic cryptographic primitives","description":"# Goal
Implement the node:crypto shim module, providing the most commonly used cryptographic functions that extensions rely on (hashing, UUID generation, random bytes).

# Background
Many extensions use crypto for content hashing (cache keys, dedup), UUID generation (unique IDs), and occasionally HMAC (webhook verification). QuickJS has no built-in crypto, so all operations must either be implemented in pure JS or routed through Rust hostcalls.

# API Surface (prioritized)

## Tier 1 — Must Have
- randomUUID() → string (v4 UUID)
- createHash(algorithm) → Hash object
  - Hash.update(data) → Hash (chainable)
  - Hash.digest(encoding) → string | Buffer
  - Algorithms: "sha256", "sha1", "md5"
- randomBytes(size) → Buffer
- randomInt(min?, max) → number

## Tier 2 — Should Have
- createHmac(algorithm, key) → Hmac object
  - Hmac.update(data) → Hmac
  - Hmac.digest(encoding) → string
- timingSafeEqual(a, b) → boolean
- getHashes() → string[] (list supported algorithms)

# Implementation Strategy
- randomUUID: Pure JS using Math.random() or delegate to Rust uuid crate via hostcall
- Hash/Hmac: Must delegate to Rust — QuickJS has no crypto primitives
  - Hostcall: pi.internal("crypto_hash", { algorithm, data, encoding })
  - Or: implement sha256/sha1/md5 in pure JS (slower but no hostcall overhead)
  - Recommendation: Rust hostcall for correctness + performance
- randomBytes: Delegate to Rust (OsRng) via hostcall
- timingSafeEqual: Must be Rust (constant-time comparison impossible in JS)

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs: Register node:crypto module
- src/extension_dispatcher.rs: Add crypto hostcall handlers (if using Rust delegation)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] randomUUID() returns valid v4 UUIDs
- [ ] createHash("sha256").update("hello").digest("hex") matches Node.js output
- [ ] randomBytes returns cryptographically secure random data
- [ ] At least sha256, sha1, md5 supported
- [ ] 10+ unit tests verifying output matches Node.js","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CobaltRobin","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:17.160270227Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T20:50:37.595248323Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T20:50:37.595092122Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crypto","extensions","shims"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:17.160270227Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1av0.4","title":"Implement node:os shim — platform info and system utilities","description":"# Goal

Extensions use node:os primarily for: platform detection (os.platform()), temp directory (os.tmpdir()), home directory (os.homedir()), and CPU info. The existing PiJS runtime (bd-2ru2) added partial os support (hostname, etc.) — this task completes the full surface.

- EOL → "\n"
- type() → "Linux" | "Darwin" | "Windows_NT"
- release() → string (kernel version)
- cpus() → Array<{ model, speed, times }>
- totalmem() → number (bytes)
- freemem() → number (bytes)

## Nice to Have
- userInfo() → { username, homedir, shell, uid, gid }
- networkInterfaces() → object
- uptime() → number (seconds)

- tmpdir/homedir: env vars or Rust std::env functions via hostcall
- cpus/totalmem/freemem: Rust sys-info via hostcall (or hardcode for sandboxed extensions)

 {"id":"bd-1av0.5","title":"Implement node:url shim — URL and URLSearchParams constructors","description":"# Goal

- url.search, url.searchParams (returns URLSearchParams)
- url.hash
- url.toString() → string
- url.toJSON() → string
- URL.canParse(input, base?) → boolean (static)

- params.append(name, value)
- params.delete(name)
- params.get(name) → string | null
- params.getAll(name) → string[]
- params.has(name) → boolean
- params.set(name, value)
- params.sort()
- params.toString() → string
- params[Symbol.iterator]() → iterator

## Legacy API (node:url specific)
- url.parse(urlString) → Url object (legacy format)
- url.format(urlObject) → string
- url.resolve(from, to) → string

-{"id":"bd-1av0.6","title":"Implement node:buffer shim — Buffer class for binary data handling","description":"# Goal
Implement the node:buffer (Buffer) shim for binary data handling, which many extensions use for encoding/decoding, file I/O, and crypto operations.

# Background
Buffer is Node.js unique binary data type. Extensions use it for: base64 encoding/decoding, hex encoding, UTF-8 string conversion, and binary file handling. QuickJS has Uint8Array but not Buffer. The shim wraps Uint8Array with Buffers additional methods.

# API Surface

## Must Have
- Buffer.from(string, encoding) → Buffer
- Buffer.from(array) → Buffer
- Buffer.from(arrayBuffer) → Buffer
- Buffer.alloc(size, fill?, encoding?) → Buffer
- Buffer.allocUnsafe(size) → Buffer (alias for alloc in safe environment)
- Buffer.isBuffer(obj) → boolean
- Buffer.byteLength(string, encoding) → number
- Buffer.concat(list, totalLength?) → Buffer
- buf.toString(encoding?, start?, end?) → string
- buf.slice(start?, end?) → Buffer
- buf.length → number
- buf.write(string, offset?, length?, encoding?) → number
- buf[index] → number (Uint8Array behavior)
- Encodings: "utf8", "utf-8", "ascii", "base64", "hex", "binary", "latin1"

## Nice to Have
- buf.compare(other) → number
- buf.equals(other) → boolean
- buf.indexOf(value) → number
- buf.includes(value) → boolean
- buf.copy(target, targetStart?, sourceStart?, sourceEnd?)
- buf.fill(value, offset?, end?, encoding?)
- buf.toJSON() → { type: "Buffer", data: [...] }

# Implementation Strategy
- Extend Uint8Array prototype with Buffer methods (pure JS)
- Buffer.from with encoding: implement base64/hex decoders in JS
- buf.toString with encoding: implement base64/hex encoders in JS
- Keep internal storage as Uint8Array for interop with other APIs
- Performance: base64/hex encode/decode in pure JS is adequate for extension use cases (not crypto-grade throughput)

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.js: Register node:buffer module, also make Buffer available as global

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Buffer.from("hello", "utf8").toString("base64") === "aGVsbG8="
- [ ] Buffer.from("aGVsbG8=", "base64").toString("utf8") === "hello"
- [ ] Buffer.from("68656c6c6f", "hex").toString("utf8") === "hello"
- [ ] Buffer.alloc(10) creates zero-filled buffer
- [ ] Buffer.isBuffer(Buffer.alloc(0)) === true
- [ ] Interop with Uint8Array (indexing, length)
- [ ] 15+ unit tests covering encodings + edge cases","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CobaltRobin","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:50.454750160Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T21:09:42.826631931Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["binary","extensions","shims"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.6","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:50.454750160Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1av0.7","title":"Implement node:events shim — EventEmitter for pub/sub patterns","description":"# Goal
Implement the node:events (EventEmitter) shim, which many extensions use for internal pub/sub communication patterns.

# Background
EventEmitter is the foundation of Nodes event-driven architecture. Many npm packages (including popular extension dependencies) inherit from EventEmitter or use it directly for event-based APIs. Pure JS implementation — no hostcalls needed.

# API Surface

## Must Have
- new EventEmitter()
- emitter.on(event, listener) → this
- emitter.once(event, listener) → this
- emitter.off(event, listener) → this (alias: removeListener)
- emitter.emit(event, ...args) → boolean
- emitter.removeAllListeners(event?) → this
- emitter.listeners(event) → Function[]
- emitter.listenerCount(event) → number
- emitter.eventNames() → (string | symbol)[]
- emitter.setMaxListeners(n) → this
- emitter.getMaxListeners() → number
- EventEmitter.defaultMaxListeners (static, default 10)
- emitter.addListener(event, listener) → this (alias for on)
- emitter.prependListener(event, listener) → this
- emitter.prependOnceListener(event, listener) → this

## Nice to Have
- events.once(emitter, name) → Promise (static helper)
- events.on(emitter, name) → AsyncIterator (static helper)

# Implementation Strategy
- Pure JavaScript class
- Internal Map<string, Function[]> for listener storage
- once() wraps listener in auto-removing wrapper
- emit() calls all listeners synchronously (matching Node behavior)
- MaxListeners warning: console.warn if exceeded (not error)

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs: Register node:events module

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] on/emit/off lifecycle works correctly
- [ ] once fires exactly once then auto-removes
- [ ] Multiple listeners on same event called in registration order
- [ ] removeAllListeners clears all or specific event
- [ ] MaxListeners warning at threshold

 {"id":"bd-1av0.8","title":"Implement node:http and node:https shims — HTTP client via pi.http hostcall","description":"# Goal
Implement node:http and node:https shims that route all HTTP requests through the capability-gated pi.http() hostcall, maintaining the security model while providing Node.js API compatibility.

# Background
Extensions that make HTTP requests (API calls, webhook delivery, data fetching) typically use either the native http/https modules or a library built on top of them (like node-fetch, axios, got). Providing http/https shims means these libraries MAY work without modification (if they only use the core request API).

Note: Many modern extensions use fetch() instead — global fetch is a separate concern (simpler to implement). This task covers the Node.js-specific http.request / https.request API.

# API Surface

## Must Have
- http.request(url | options, callback?) → ClientRequest
- http.get(url | options, callback?) → ClientRequest
- https.request(url | options, callback?) → ClientRequest
- https.get(url | options, callback?) → ClientRequest

## ClientRequest object
- req.write(chunk) — write request body
- req.end(chunk?) — finish request
- req.on("response", (res) => {}) — response event
- req.on("error", (err) => {}) — error event
- req.abort() / req.destroy() — cancel

## IncomingMessage (response) object
- res.statusCode → number
- res.headers → object
- res.on("data", (chunk) => {}) — body chunks
- res.on("end", () => {}) — body complete

## Options object
- hostname, port, path, method, headers, timeout

# Implementation Strategy
- ClientRequest accumulates body chunks via write()
- On end(): send pi.http() hostcall with accumulated body
- Response: parse hostcall result into IncomingMessage
- Streaming response: depends on streaming hostcall epic (bd-2tl1)
  - Without streaming: buffer full response, emit "data" + "end" immediately
  - With streaming: emit "data" events as StreamChunks arrive
- EventEmitter-based (depends on node:events shim)

# Dependencies
- Depends on node:events shim (for EventEmitter inheritance)
- Benefits from streaming hostcall (bd-2tl1) for true streaming responses
- Without streaming hostcall: still functional but buffers full response

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs: Register node:http and node:https modules

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] http.get("http://example.com", (res) => { ... }) works
- [ ] https.request with POST body works
- [ ] Response headers and status code accessible
- [ ] Response body delivered via data/end events
- [ ] Error events on network failure
- [ ] Request timeout works
- [ ] All requests go through pi.http hostcall (capability-gated)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:12.543933407Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:19.702013687Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","http","shims"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:12.543933407Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.8","depends_on_id":"bd-1av0.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:17.547833643Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1av0.8","depends_on_id":"bd-2tl1","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:19.701950349Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1av0.9","title":"Implement process global shim — env, cwd, platform, exit, stdout, pid","description":"# Goal
Implement the global process object that nearly every Node.js extension expects. This is the SINGLE MOST CRITICAL shim — 315 uses of process.env, 139 of process.cwd(), 70 of process.exit(), 62 of process.platform across the conformance corpus.

# Background (DATA-DRIVEN)
Analysis of 200+ extensions in tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/ shows process.* is used more than ANY other Node.js API:
  process.env          315 occurrences (config, API keys, feature flags)
  process.cwd          139 occurrences (resolve relative paths)
  process.exit          70 occurrences (fatal error handling)
  process.platform      62 occurrences (platform detection)
  process.stdout        44 occurrences (output streaming)
  process.pid           33 occurrences (process identification)
  process.argv          23 occurrences (CLI argument parsing)
  process.kill          17 occurrences (signal sending)
  process.stdin         15 occurrences (input reading)
  process.on            13 occurrences (event handlers)
  process.execPath      12 occurrences (binary path)
  process.arch           3 occurrences (architecture detection)

Without this shim, the MAJORITY of extensions fail on the very first line that reads process.env.

# API Surface (prioritized by usage)

## Tier 1 — Must Have (blocks 80%+ of extensions)
- process.env → Proxy object that reads from Rust env vars via hostcall
  - CRITICAL: Must support process.env.HOME, process.env.PATH, etc.
  - Must be a Proxy so dynamic property access works (not just pre-defined keys)
  - Security: filtered by extension policy (some env vars blocked)
- process.cwd() → string (current working directory via hostcall)
- process.platform → "linux" | "darwin" | "win32" (compile-time constant)
- process.exit(code?) → void (requests extension shutdown with code)
- process.pid → number (QuickJS thread ID or synthetic)
- process.argv → string[] (["pi", extension_name])

## Tier 2 — Should Have (blocks 20-40% of extensions)
- process.stdout → writable stream stub { write(data) }
  - Route to console.log internally
  - Needed by extensions that pipe output
- process.stderr → writable stream stub { write(data) }
- process.on("exit", callback) → register cleanup handler
- process.on("uncaughtException", callback) → error handler
- process.kill(pid, signal) → hostcall to Rust (capability-gated)
- process.execPath → "/usr/bin/pi" (path to Pi binary)
- process.arch → "x64" | "arm64" (compile-time)
- process.version → "v20.0.0" (synthetic Node.js version for compat)
- process.versions → { node: "20.0.0", v8: "n/a", ... }

# Implementation Strategy
- process is a GLOBAL object, not a module import — register it in QuickJS global scope at runtime init
- process.env: Use ES6 Proxy to intercept property access → hostcall to Rust std::env::var()
  - Cache env vars for the lifetime of the extension (snapshot at load time)
  - Writes to process.env: store locally, dont mutate real env
- process.cwd(): Single hostcall at init, cache result
- process.exit(): Request extension shutdown via runtime signal (dont call std::process::exit!)
- process.stdout/stderr: Lightweight writable stream that calls console.log/console.error

# Security Considerations
- process.env MUST filter sensitive vars (API keys, secrets) based on extension policy
- process.kill MUST be capability-gated (deny by default)
- process.exit should NOT kill the Pi process — only the extension context

# Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs: Register process global at QuickJS context creation
- src/extension_dispatcher.rs: Add env-read hostcall handler

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] process.env.HOME returns home directory
- [ ] process.env.PATH returns PATH
- [ ] process.env.NONEXISTENT returns undefined
- [ ] process.cwd() returns current working directory
- [ ] process.platform returns correct platform string
- [ ] process.exit(0) cleanly terminates extension without crashing Pi
- [ ] process.stdout.write("hello") outputs to console

 {"id":"bd-1yfi","title":"Design shared mock specification format (JSON schema)","description":"# Design shared mock specification format (JSON schema)

## Context
Both TS and Rust runtimes need identical mock behavior. The mock spec is a JSON file that defines exactly what each hostcall should return.

## Mock Spec Design

### HTTP Mocks
Match by method + URL pattern. Return deterministic status/headers/body.
Support: exact URL match, prefix match, regex match.

### Exec Mocks
Match by command + args pattern. Return deterministic stdout/stderr/exit_code.
Support: exact match, prefix match.

### Session State
Pre-defined: messages array, session name, current model, thinking level, active tools.
These are what getMessages(), getSessionName(), etc. return.

### UI Capture
Instead of rendering, capture all UI calls as structured events:
- showSpinner(text) -> {type: "spinner_show", text: "..."}
- updateSpinner(text) -> {type: "spinner_update", text: "..."}
- stopSpinner() -> {type: "spinner_stop"}
- showConfirm/showDialog -> auto-accept with configured response

### Event Payloads
Standard event payloads for: tool_call, tool_result, turn_start, turn_end, before_agent_start, input, context, resources_discover, user_bash, session_before_compact, session_before_tree.

## Output
- docs/schema/mock_spec.json (JSON Schema)
- tests/ext_conformance/fixtures/mock_spec_default.json (default mock spec)
- tests/ext_conformance/fixtures/mock_spec_with_http.json (example with HTTP mocks)

- JSON Schema validates mock spec files
- Default mock spec covers all hostcall types
- Example specs demonstrate each mock category","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:20:21.034442792Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T07:48:49.229054197Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T07:48:49.228987463Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1yfi","depends_on_id":"bd-6koq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:20:21.049487082Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":327,"issue_id":"bd-1yfi","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Mock spec complete.  {"id":"bd-1yo9","title":"Feature: Extension Tool Registration and Execution","description":"# Feature: Extension Tool Registration and Execution

## Overview

Extensions can register custom tools via pi.registerTool(). These tools must be:
1. Added to the agent's tool registry
2. Included in the tools array sent to the provider
3. Executable when the model invokes them

## Current State

The JS-side registration exists:
- `pi.registerTool(config)` registers a tool definition
- `__pi_execute_tool(name, callId, input)` exists in PI_BRIDGE_JS
- Tool definitions stored in extension's tools map

What's missing:
- Rust-side collection of registered tools
- Integration with agent's ToolRegistry
- Execution flow from model tool call → JS handler → result

## Architecture

```
Extension calls pi.registerTool()
            │
            ▼
JS runtime stores tool definition
            │
            ▼
Agent collects tools from all extensions ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Tools added to ToolRegistry ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Model receives tools in API call
            │
            ▼
Model invokes extension tool
            │
            ▼
Agent routes to extension handler ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
JS __pi_execute_tool() called ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Result returned to model
```

## Implementation Requirements

### 1. Collect Registered Tools
After extension activation, query JS runtime for registered tools:
```rust
let ext_tools = runtime.get_registered_tools()?;
```

### 2. Create Tool Wrapper
Wrap each extension tool as an AgentTool:
```rust
struct ExtensionToolWrapper {
    name: String,
    label: String,
    description: String,
    parameters: serde_json::Value,
    runtime: Arc<PiJsRuntime>,
}

#[async_trait]
impl Tool for ExtensionToolWrapper {
    async fn execute(&self, call_id: &str, input: Value) -> Result<ToolOutput> {
        self.runtime.execute_extension_tool(&self.name, call_id, input).await
    }
}
```

### 3. Add to Registry
```rust
for tool_def in runtime.get_registered_tools()? {
    let wrapper = ExtensionToolWrapper::new(tool_def, runtime.clone());
    tool_registry.register(wrapper);
}
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Extension tools appear in provider's tools array
- [ ] Model can invoke extension tools
- [ ] Tool execution routes to JS handler
- [ ] Results return to model correctly
- [ ] Errors handled properly","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.168079003Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T22:43:18.577991006Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T22:43:18.577924021Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1yo9","depends_on_id":"bd-30zg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.174573255Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
